content.thinking:
So `documentation.json` IS tracked in git (shown by `git ls-files` returning a result). The root `.gitignore` doesn't ignore it. 

Now I have a full picture:

**Finding 1** (Important): `project.json:72` - `build-storybook` target has `"browserTarget": "ngx-smart-components:build-storybook"` (self-reference). Fix: change to `"ngx-smart-components:build"` since the project has a `build` target (visible in project.json).

**Finding 2** (Important): `preview.ts:3` - static import of `documentation.json` uses `setCompodocJson` from `@storybook/addon-docs/angular`. 

The real issue is that the static TypeScript import requires the file to exist at build time, even though `documentation.json` is already tracked as a stub in git. I need to switch from a static import to a dynamic require or import statement that can gracefully handle the file's absence. The stub is already committed, so the immediate compilation issue is resolved, but the real concern is whether the static import unnecessarily couples TypeScript to Compodoc's build order—though the stub mitigates this risk. The review suggests documenting the stub approach in the README so contributors understand the setup, or alternatively switching to dynamic imports for cleaner decoupling. I should verify whether `setCompodocJson` is actually available in the installed Storybook version. The fix for Finding 2 is really about clarifying the pattern rather than changing the import itself. Now I need to verify a few key things: whether `browserTarget` pointing to `ngx-smart-components:build` actually works with `@storybook/angular` when the build target uses `@nx/angular:package` instead of the expected `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`, what the correct import path is for the `fn()` function in Storybook args, and what's happening with the `await` in Finding 4.

Looking at the testing library behavior, `expect()` is synchronous, so that's confirmed. For the `browserTarget` issue specifically, since this is an Angular library rather than an app, there's no browser build target available—the `build` target uses the package executor. The real question is whether Storybook can work without `browserTarget` entirely or if there's a different configuration needed for libraries.

content.text: `documentation.json` is tracked (clean working tree). Now verifying Class 2 questions: `browserTarget` semantics for `@storybook/angular` with an Nx Angular library, and the `fn()` import for Storybook play tests.

Web search results for query: "@storybook/angular browserTarget Nx Angular library project.json 2025 2026 no app target"

Links: [{"title":"Set up Storybook for Angular Projects | Nx","url":"https://nx.dev/docs/technologies/test-tools/storybook/guides/overview-angular"},{"title":"The `browserTarget` for Angular projects with a Storybook configuration | Nx","url":"https://nx-byy86tl1r-nrwl.vercel.app/storybook/angular-browser-target"},{"title":"Running @storybook/angular:start-storybook with Storybook 8 fails to load preset · Issue #26531 · nrwl/nx","url":"https://github.com/nrwl/nx/issues/26531"},{"title":"Fixing \"No Projects Support the Serve Target\" Error in Angular: Complete 2026 Guide - Projects support the serve target error in angular complete","url":"https://copyprogramming.com/howto/no-projects-support-the-serve-target-stuck-and-can-t-run-angular-project-on-my-localhost"},{"title":"Storybook v.7 build fail · Issue #16222 · nrwl/nx","url":"https://github.com/nrwl/nx/issues/16222"},{"title":"Help: How can I run Storybook 7+ inside an Angular library · storybookjs/storybook · Discussion #20808","url":"https://github.com/storybookjs/storybook/discussions/20808"},{"title":"Storybook 7 with Angular 15 and browserTarget · storybookjs/storybook · Discussion #21462","url":"https://github.com/storybookjs/storybook/discussions/21462"},{"title":"Storybook for Angular | Storybook docs","url":"https://storybook.js.org/docs/get-started/frameworks/angular"},{"title":"Configuring Tailwind CSS for Storybook in an Angular Library inside an NX monorepo - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/codewithahsan/configuring-tailwind-css-for-storybook-in-an-angular-library-inside-an-nx-monorepo-4jbd"},{"title":"Cannot find target storybook for project · Issue #10039 · nrwl/nx","url":"https://github.com/nrwl/nx/issues/10039"}]

Here is a comprehensive summary of the search results related to **`@storybook/angular` `browserTarget`**, **Nx Angular library**, **`project.json`**, and the **"no app target"** problem:

---

## 🔍 `@storybook/angular` `browserTarget` in Nx Angular Library — Key Findings

### 1. 📖 What is `browserTarget`?

`browserTarget` is specified for the `storybook` and `build-storybook` targets in Nx, much like it is done for `serve` or other targets. Angular needs the `browserTarget` for Storybook in order to know which configuration to use for the build.

---

### 2. 🏗️ Buildable vs. Non-Buildable Libraries

If your project is **buildable** (it has a `build` target, and uses the main Angular builder — `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`, `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application`, or `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser-esbuild`) the `browserTarget` for Storybook will use the **`build` target**. If it's **not buildable** (or is using another Angular builder), it will use the **`build-storybook` target**.

---

### 3. ✅ Nx Handles This Automatically

You do not have to do anything manually — Nx will create the configuration for you. Even if you are migrating from an older version of Nx, Nx will make sure to change your `project.json` Storybook targets to match the new schema.

---

### 4. 📄 Example `project.json` Configuration (Non-Buildable Library)

When a library has no dedicated `build` target (no app target), the `browserTarget` should **self-reference** the `build-storybook` target. Here's a real-world example from a GitHub issue:


```json
"storybook": {
  "executor": "@storybook/angular:start-storybook",
  "options": {
    "port": 4400,
    "configDir": "libs/shared/ui/.storybook",
    "browserTarget": "shared-ui:build-storybook",
    "compodoc": false
  }
},
"build-storybook": {
  "executor": "@storybook/angular:build-storybook",
  "outputs": ["{options.outputDir}"],
  "options": {
    "outputDir": "dist/storybook/shared-ui",
    "configDir": "libs/shared/ui/.storybook",
    "browserTarget": "shared-ui:build-storybook",
    "compodoc": false
  }
}
```


> 💡 Notice that **both** `storybook` and `build-storybook` point `browserTarget` to `shared-ui:build-storybook` — the library's own `build-storybook` target — since there's no app `build` target.

---

### 5. 🧩 Angular Library with No `build` Target — Adding a Dummy `serve-storybook` Target

For Angular libraries that have **no app-like `build` target**, one community-found workaround is to manually add a `serve-storybook` target using `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`:


```json
"serve-storybook": {
  "builder": "@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser",
  "options": {
    "tsConfig": ".storybook/tsconfig.json",
    "assets": [],
    "index": "src/index.html",
    "main": "src/main.ts",
    "outputPath": "../app/src/main/webapp/",
    "styles": []
  }
},
"storybook": {
  "builder": "@storybook/angular:start-storybook",
  "options": {
    "configDir": ".storybook",
    "browserTarget": "my-custom-library:serve-storybook",
    "compodoc": false,
    "port": 6006,
    "styles": []
  }
}
```


---

### 6. ⚠️ Common Pitfall: `compodoc` Causing Silent Build Failures

If `"compodoc": true`, or if `compodoc` is absent from the build configuration, **the build doesn't happen**. Make sure to explicitly set `"compodoc": false` if you're not using it.

---

### 7. 🗂️ Nx Library with No Config in `project.json`

When working with an Angular **library** (not an app) there's no need for `browserTarget` in the traditional sense — you're working with an Angular library which needs to work with Storybook, and that Storybook needs the global styles for parsing directives.

There may be no configuration in `project.json` for the library at all, which can be confusing — especially because there's nothing listed as `mylib:storybook` either.

---

### 📌 TL;DR Summary

| Scenario | `browserTarget` Value |
|---|---|
| **Buildable library** (has `build` target) | `my-lib:build` |
| **Non-buildable library** (no app target) | `my-lib:build-storybook` (self-reference) |
| **Library with custom dummy target** | `my-lib:serve-storybook` |

The core issue is that `@storybook/angular` requires a valid Angular build target. For Nx Angular **libraries** that are not full applications, the `browserTarget` must point to either a self-referencing `build-storybook` target or a manually created `serve-storybook`-style dummy target.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "storybook angular fn import args play test @storybook/test 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Interactive Angular Component Testing with Storybook and Vitest - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/brandontroberts/interactive-angular-component-testing-with-storybook-and-vitest-1890"},{"title":"Interaction tests | Storybook docs","url":"https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/interaction-testing"},{"title":"@storybook/testing-angular | Storybook integrations","url":"https://storybook.js.org/addons/@storybook/testing-angular"},{"title":"Component tests | Storybook docs - JS.ORG","url":"https://storybook.js.org/docs/8/writing-tests/component-testing"},{"title":"@storybook/testing-angular - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@storybook/testing-angular"},{"title":"@storybook/test - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@storybook/test"},{"title":"Storybook: Frontend workshop for UI development","url":"https://storybook.js.org/docs/essentials/actions"},{"title":"Build a simple component | Storybook Tutorials","url":"https://storybook.js.org/tutorials/intro-to-storybook/angular/en/simple-component/"},{"title":"How to test UIs with Storybook | Storybook docs","url":"https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests"},{"title":"A guide to Storybook Test for interaction testing - LogRocket Blog","url":"https://blog.logrocket.com/guide-storybook-test-interaction-testing/"}]

Here is a comprehensive summary of what was found regarding **Storybook Angular**, `fn`, `args`, `play`, and `@storybook/test` in 2025:

---

## 🧪 Storybook Angular: Interactive Testing with `@storybook/test`

### 📦 Core Import Pattern

The standard way to set up a story for Angular with interaction testing looks like this:

```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/angular';
import { fn, expect, userEvent, within } from '@storybook/test';
import { Button } from './button.component';

const meta: Meta<Button> = {
  title: 'Example/Button',
  component: Button,
  args: { onClick: fn() },
};
export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<Button>;

export const Primary: Story = {
  args: { primary: true, label: 'Button' },
  play: async ({ canvasElement, args }) => {
    const canvas = within(canvasElement);
    const button = canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /Button/i });
    await userEvent.click(button);
    await expect(args.onClick).toHaveBeenCalled();
  },
};
```


> **Note:** As of the latest Storybook versions (2025), the import path may shift from `@storybook/test` to `storybook/test`. Both are referenced across official docs.

---

### 🔍 What is `fn()`?

Actions work via supplying special Storybook-generated mock functions to your story's event handler args. The recommended way to write actions is to use the `fn` utility from `storybook/test` to mock and spy on args — this is very useful for writing interaction tests.

`fn()` allows you to create a callback that appears in the **Actions panel** of the Storybook UI when clicked. So when building a button, you can determine if a button click is successful in the UI.

---

### ▶️ The `play` Function

Play functions can be used for setting up state, creating spies, mocking out the network, simulating user interactions with your components, asserting output, and more. They are the foundation for testing in Storybook. Here's a more complex example which includes spying and mocking via the `fn` utility.

When your component calls a function, you can spy on that function to make assertions on its behavior using the `fn` utility from Vitest, available via the `storybook/test` module. Most of the time, you will use `fn` as an arg value when writing your story, then access that arg in your test.

---

### 🔄 `fn()` Mock Auto-Restore

It is not necessary to restore `fn()` mocks, as Storybook will already do that automatically before rendering a story. See the `parameters.test.restoreMocks` API for more information.

---

### 🔧 `@storybook/test` API Overview

Under the hood, Storybook's `@storybook/test` package provides **Testing Library's** `user-events` APIs. If you're familiar with Testing Library, you should be at home in Storybook.

Storybook's `@storybook/test` also provides APIs from **Vitest**, such as `expect` and `vi.fn`. These APIs improve your testing experience, helping you assert whether a function has been called, if an element exists in the DOM, and much more.

Note that `expect`, `within`, and `userEvent` testing utilities now come from the **single `@storybook/test` package** rather than the separate `@vitest/spy`, `@storybook/testing-library`, and `@storybook/jest` packages.

---

### ⚙️ Angular + Vitest Integration (2025)

Storybook with Vitest also enables interactive component testing. A dedicated post guides you through setting up Storybook with Angular using Vite and the `@storybook/addon-vitest` package for interactive component testing.

You can install the AnalogJS Storybook Angular integration with:
```bash
npm install @analogjs/storybook-angular @angular/animations zone.js --save-dev
```


---

### ♻️ Reusing Stories in Angular Tests (`@storybook/testing-angular`)

`@storybook/testing-angular` is a solution to **reuse your Storybook stories in your Angular tests**. By reusing your stories in your tests, you have a catalog of component scenarios ready to be tested. All args and decorators from your story and its meta, and also global decorators, will be composed by this library. In your unit tests, all you have to do is select which story you want to render, and all the necessary setup will be already done for you.

This library requires you to be using **Storybook version 8**, Component Story Format (CSF), and hoisted CSF annotations, which is the recommended way to write stories since Storybook 8.

---

This covers the key patterns and APIs for **Storybook Angular interactive testing** using `fn`, `args`, `play`, and `@storybook/test` as of 2025. Let me know if you'd like a deeper dive into any specific part!


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.thinking:
Excellent. Now I have very important information about `browserTarget`:

**Finding 1 clarification**: For an Angular library that has NO `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser` target, the correct `browserTarget` IS the `build-storybook` self-reference. The `ngx-smart-components` `build` target uses `@nx/angular:package` (an Angular library packager, not a browser builder). The Nx documentation says:
- For **buildable libraries** (with `build` target using Angular browser builder) -> `my-lib:build`


- For **non-buildable libraries** (no app target) -> `my-lib:build-storybook` (self-reference)

Since `ngx-smart-components:build` uses `@nx/angular:package` rather than a browser builder, setting `browserTarget` to the self-reference `ngx-smart-components:build-storybook` follows the standard Nx pattern for libraries without a dedicated browser target. This suggests the review report's Finding 1 might actually be incorrect—the self-reference is the appropriate configuration here.

Now I need to check the Nx documentation to confirm this pattern. Looking at the import paths in Finding 3 and 4, the stories file already imports `expect` from `storybook/test` on line 3, so `fn` should follow the same import path rather than using `@storybook/test`. For Finding 4, the `await` before `expect()` is unnecessary since `toBeTruthy()` is synchronous, and the test logic itself seems flawed—`getByText` throws when an element isn't found, so checking `toBeTruthy()` on a successful result is redundant.

The real issue is whether the project's `build` target using `@nx/angular:package` qualifies as a valid browserTarget for Storybook to inherit styles and assets, since the Nx guide distinguishes between buildable libraries with a `build` target and non-buildable ones.

Looking at the Nx classification more carefully, `ngx-smart-components` doesn't actually qualify as a "buildable library" in Storybook's sense because its `build` target uses `@nx/angular:package` rather than one of the Angular browser builders like `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`. This means the self-reference to `build-storybook` is actually the correct pattern, which directly contradicts the review's claim that it was a copy-paste error.

content.content:
Based on the provided documentation, the guide does not specifically address the `browserTarget` configuration for libraries using the `@nx/angular:package` builder.

The documentation states: "If your project is buildable (it has a `build` target, and uses the main Angular builder...) the `browserTarget` for Storybook will use the `build` target, if it's not buildable (or is using another Angular builder) it will use the `build-storybook` target."

However, the `@nx/angular:package` builder is not mentioned as one of the supported Angular builders listed. The guide indicates that "Nx will create the configuration for you," suggesting automatic handling, but it does not explicitly clarify whether self-reference to `build-storybook` is valid for package-based libraries.

For a definitive answer regarding package builder libraries, you would need to consult the official Nx Angular documentation or the Storybook configuration guidelines beyond this guide's scope.

However, the `@nx/angular:package` builder is not mentioned as one of the supported Angular builders listed. The guide indicates that "Nx will create the configuration for you," suggesting automatic handling, but it does not explicitly clarify whether self-reference to `build-storybook` is valid for package-based libraries.

content.thinking:
The Nx docs confirm that `@nx/angular:package` is NOT one of the supported "main Angular builders" (which are `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`, `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application`, or `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser-esbuild`). Therefore, the documentation explicitly states that for projects "not buildable (or using another Angular builder), it will use the `build-storybook` target." 

This means the self-reference to `build-storybook` is the CORRECT pattern for this library. Finding 1 of the review report ("browserTarget self-references build-storybook target -- Per pv-3 schema expects @angular-devkit/build-angular:browser target; set to ngx-smart-components:build or omit (default null)") appears to be INCORRECT.
 
The review claims the schema expects `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`, but this project uses `@nx/angular:package` instead, which means the Nx docs explicitly allow the `build-storybook` self-reference. I need to check whether the `browserTarget` field is actually present in the `storybook` target or only in `build-storybook`.

Looking more carefully at the project.json, the `storybook` target (lines 43-65) doesn't have a `browserTarget` key at all, while `build-storybook` (lines 66-88) has `"browserTarget": "ngx-smart-components:build-storybook"` on line 72. The Nx docs show examples where both targets have this field, but the current setup only has it on `build-storybook`, which might be a slight deviation from the documented pattern.

Now I'm reconsidering the review's feedback. The review claims the self-referential `browserTarget` is a copy-paste error and suggests changing it to `ngx-smart-components:build`, but that would actually be wrong. Since `ngx-smart-components:build` uses `@nx/angular:package` (a library packager, not a browser builder), Storybook can't inherit styles and assets from it. The Nx documentation explicitly states that for libraries using `@nx/angular:package`, the `browserTarget` should point to `build-storybook`, which is exactly what's configured here. The review's recommendation contradicts the documented correct pattern for this scenario.

For Finding 2, the static import issue is more nuanced—the file is tracked as a stub in the commit history, so I need to understand the full context before deciding whether this coupling is actually problematic.

For Finding 3, I can address the args issue by setting up the story with proper control values and using the test utilities for the output handler.

For Finding 4, I'm partway through evaluating...

Actually, looking at the next thinking, I see the issue with the test assertion—`getByText` already throws if the element isn't found, so wrapping it in `toBeTruthy()` is redundant. I should remove the `await` since `expect()` is synchronous, and instead use `toBeInTheDocument()` if that matcher is available, or switch to `findByText` for async queries. I need to verify whether `@testing-library/jest-dom` is set up in the project.

For Finding 5, there's a stray React webpack documentation comment in the Angular config that should just be deleted since it doesn't apply here.

On the `documentation.json` issue—it's currently tracked in git even though the reviewer flagged it as something that should be ignored. But looking at the commit history, this was intentionally kept as a stub for fresh clones, so the right move is to keep it tracked and add a note to the README explaining why rather than adding it to `.gitignore`.

Before I finalize the fix plan, I need to ask the advisor two things: whether Finding 1's self-reference is actually correct for an `@nx/angular:package` library, and what the optimal sequence is for addressing the remaining findings.

I should also verify whether `@testing-library/jest-dom` is already installed, since that affects how to handle Finding 4.

`@testing-library/jest-dom` is NOT installed. That means `toBeInTheDocument()` is not available! This affects Finding 4.

Without `@testing-library/jest-dom`, the fix for Finding 4 should be:
- Remove `await` (it's synchronous)
- Change `toBeTruthy()` to something that actually validates the element - options:
  - Keep `getByText` which throws on miss (implicit assertion), but drop the `.toBeTruthy()` check or add a more explicit check


- Use `expect(canvas.getByText(...)).not.toBeNull()` - this works without jest-dom, or just rely on `getByText` throwing as the implicit assertion

Since `expect` is imported from `storybook/test` which provides Vitest-compatible assertions, the simplest fix without adding a new dependency is to validate the element exists using `.not.toBeNull()` instead of relying on jest-dom matchers.

Now I'm compiling the key findings for the advisor, starting with the browserTarget self-reference pattern...

the Nx documentation confirms that's actually correct for `@nx/angular:package` libraries, so the review's suggestion to change it is wrong. The static import issue is just documentation since the stub is already tracked. For the args object, I need to add the `fn` import from `storybook/test` and set up the args properly. The toBeTruthy matcher won't work without `@testing-library/jest-dom`, so I have a few options there—either install it as a devDependency, switch to `not.toBeNull()` which works with just Vitest, or drop the expect entirely since `getByText` throws on failure anyway. I should also delete that React URL comment and document the documentation.json stub in the README. Let me double-check whether the missing `browserTarget` on the storybook target itself is actually a problem by looking at the project configuration again.

input.content:
# Plan: Fix Review Findings (Compodoc + Storybook Integration)

## Strategic Direction

1. Reject Finding 1. Per `pv-nx-browsertarget`, self-reference is correct for `@nx/angular:package` libs. Document rejection in `plans/fix-review-findings.plan.md`.
2. Address Finding 2 docs-only. Add README section in `packages/ngx-smart-components/README.md` explaining committed `documentation.json` stub and Compodoc regeneration.
3. Address Finding 3. Set `Primary.args` to `{ label: 'NgxSmartComponents works!' }`; add `clicked: fn()` to both stories; import `fn` from `storybook/test`.
4. Address Finding 4. Drop `await` and `.toBeTruthy()`; keep `canvas.getByText(...)` standalone (throws on miss).
5. Address Finding 5. Delete comment block at `main.ts:14-16` entirely.
6. Reject gitignore surface. Committed stub is deliberate; note rationale in plan.

## Findings Disposition

- **Finding F1:** `project.json:72` -- `build-storybook.browserTarget` self-references `ngx-smart-components:build-storybook`
- **Disposition:** rejected
- **Rationale:** Nx documentation explicitly states that for libraries whose `build` target does not use `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser/application/browser-esbuild`, the correct `browserTarget` is the `build-storybook` self-reference. This library's `build` target uses `@nx/angular:package` (a library packager, not a browser builder), so the self-reference is the correct and documented pattern. Setting it to `ngx-smart-components:build` would point Storybook at a package builder incapable of providing styles/assets.

- **Finding F2:** `preview.ts:3` -- static import of `documentation.json` couples TypeScript compilation to Compodoc build order
- **Disposition:** addressed (Step 1)
- **Rationale:** The stub fix is already in place (commit `20b5779`). Only documentation is missing. Step 1 adds a README section explaining the pattern.

- **Finding F3:** `stories.ts:14-16` -- `args: {}` hides `label` control and `clicked` action from the Controls/Actions panel
- **Disposition:** addressed (Step 2)
- **Rationale:** Exposing args is necessary for the story to demonstrate Compodoc integration as documented in the component's JSDoc.

- **Finding F4:** `stories.ts:21` -- `await expect(...).toBeTruthy()` is redundant and has unnecessary `await`
- **Disposition:** addressed (Step 2)
- **Rationale:** `getByText` already throws on miss, making the explicit assertion vacuous. The `await` is incorrect on a synchronous `expect()`. `@testing-library/jest-dom` is not installed so `toBeInTheDocument()` is not available; the standalone `getByText` call is the correct minimal fix.

- **Finding F5:** `main.ts:14-16` -- comment block references React webpack docs URL in an Angular project
- **Disposition:** addressed (Step 3)
- **Rationale:** The comment adds no value; deleting it is cleaner than replacing the URL.

- **Missed surface (gitignore):** `documentation.json` is tracked but `.gitignore` does not list it
- **Disposition:** rejected
- **Rationale:** The committed stub IS the deliberate solution (commit `20b5779`). The "contradiction" is intentional -- the file must be tracked so fresh clones compile without running Compodoc first. This is documented in Step 1.

## Steps

1. **Document the `documentation.json` stub pattern in README**
   - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/README.md` (create if absent)
   - Change: Add a "Storybook development" section explaining: (a) `documentation.json` is committed as an empty stub so `nx storybook ngx-smart-components` works on a fresh clone without running Compodoc first; (b) Compodoc regenerates it automatically when Storybook starts (via the `compodocArgs` in `project.json`); (c) the file will show as modified after a Storybook run -- this is expected; contributors can run `git checkout packages/ngx-smart-components/documentation.json` to reset it or leave it as-is.
   - Rationale: Closes Finding 2's documentation gap; also explains the gitignore non-issue for new contributors.

2. **Fix story args and play function in `ngx-smart-components.stories.ts`**
   - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/src/lib/ngx-smart-components/ngx-smart-components.stories.ts`
   - Change:
     - Add `fn` to the import from `storybook/test` (line 3 currently imports only `expect`).
     - Set `Primary.args` to `{ label: 'NgxSmartComponents works!', clicked: fn() }`.
     - Set `Heading.args` to `{ label: 'NgxSmartComponents works!', clicked: fn() }`.
     - In the `Heading` play function, replace `await expect(canvas.getByText(/NgxSmartComponents works!/gi)).toBeTruthy()` with the standalone call `canvas.getByText(/NgxSmartComponents works!/gi);` (no `await`, no `expect`, no `toBeTruthy`).
   - Rationale: Addresses Finding 3 (exposes label control and clicked action) and Finding 4 (removes vacuous assertion and incorrect await).

3. **Delete the React webpack comment block from `main.ts`**
   - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/.storybook/main.ts`
   - Change: Delete lines 14-16 (the comment block starting with `// To customize your webpack configuration...`).
   - Rationale: Addresses Finding 5; the React URL is misleading and the Nx recipe link on the second line is the only useful reference, but in context it adds no actionable guidance.

## Key Decisions

- **Finding 1 rejection**: The review's pv-3 claim that `browserTarget` should point at `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser` does not apply here. `@nx/angular:package` is not a browser builder. The Nx docs' "non-buildable or using another Angular builder" rule applies, making the self-reference correct. Changing to `ngx-smart-components:build` would be wrong.
- **Finding 4 fix approach**: Using standalone `getByText` (no `expect` wrapper) rather than installing `@testing-library/jest-dom` or switching to `not.toBeNull()`. This avoids a new dependency and is idiomatic -- `getByText` is already an assertion by contract (throws on miss).
- **Finding 2 scope**: No code change needed. The static import + committed stub works correctly on fresh clones. Documentation is the only gap.

## Dependencies

- Steps 1, 2, and 3 are fully independent and can be done in any order or in parallel.

## Verdict

**Verdict scope:** scope: api-correctness

The plan covers Storybook/Angular story correctness, Compodoc integration documentation, and test assertion correctness. It does NOT cover security threats, performance, or accessibility. Downstream skills reading this plan MUST check scope-match before treating this verdict as authoritative for other question axes.
